questions.a: A variable-length encoding for Unicode text.
Encoding binary data as text-safe characters.
Converting in-memory data into a storable or transmissible format.
A text-based data interchange format using key-value structures and arrays.
A compression format and algorithm commonly used for reducing text payloads.

questions.q: Why can 0.1 + 0.2 be surprising in floating point?
questions.a: Binary floating point cannot represent many decimal fractions exactly.

questions.q: Why is UTF-8 popular on the web?
questions.a: It is backward-compatible with ASCII and efficient for common text.

questions.q: Why are binary formats often smaller than JSON?
questions.a: They omit field names and store values in compact encoded forms.

questions.q: What problem does schema evolution solve?
questions.a: It lets producers and consumers change over time without breaking old data.

questions.q: Why is compression helpful for network payloads?
questions.a: It reduces bytes on the wire and can lower transfer time when CPU cost is acceptable.

questions.q: What is the difference between encoding and compression?
questions.a: Encoding changes representation; compression reduces size.

questions.q: Why do some systems choose protobuf over JSON?
questions.a: They want smaller payloads, faster parsing, and explicit schemas.

questions.q: When would you choose a text format over a binary format?
questions.a: When human readability, debugging, and ease of integration matter more than compactness.

questions.q: What tradeoff does compression add to a request pipeline?
questions.a: It saves bandwidth but adds CPU work and sometimes latency.

questions.q: Why can careless schema changes break consumers?
questions.a: Readers may depend on field names, types, or required fields that are no longer present.

questions.q: When is Base64 the wrong solution?
questions.a: When you can send raw binary and avoid the 33 percent size overhead.

questions.q: What tradeoff do self-describing formats like JSON introduce?
questions.a: They are easy to inspect, but they cost more bytes and parse time than compact binary encodings.

questions.q: Why does exact decimal money handling often avoid binary floating point?
questions.a: Because rounding error can accumulate, so fixed-point or decimal types are safer.
